summary:
Nicolaus Copernicus's Heliocentric Theory (De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium, 1543 AD) proposed that the Earth and other planets orbit the Sun rather than the Sun orbiting the Earth — a shift that initiated the [[event:104|Scientific Revolution]] and what later became known as the 'Copernican Revolution' in astronomy and philosophy. Copernicus, a Polish canon and astronomer, developed the heliocentric model over decades of mathematical and observational work, reportedly receiving the first printed copy of his book on his deathbed. His theory preserved the Greek preference for circular orbits and uniform motion (producing predictions no more accurate than Ptolemy's geocentric system), but it fundamentally challenged the Earth's central, privileged position in the cosmos and provided the mathematical framework that [[event:2474|Kepler]] would later improve with elliptical orbits.

The Copernican model's reception was slow and contested. Many astronomers initially treated it as a mathematical device for calculations rather than a physical description of reality — a reading facilitated by an unauthorized preface added by the publisher (without Copernicus's knowledge) suggesting as much. The Catholic Church initially showed limited concern — Cardinal Schönberg had encouraged Copernicus to publish — but placed De Revolutionibus on the Index of Forbidden Books in 1616 (after Galileo's advocacy made the issue contentious). The full implications of Copernicanism — that Earth was not the center of creation, that the cosmos was perhaps infinite, and that biblical cosmology was not scientifically authoritative — were worked out by thinkers like Giordano Bruno (burned for heresy in 1600, partly for cosmological views) and [[event:2472|Galileo]] over the century after Copernicus's death.
